Under-estimating morbidity (leading to under-pricing) is more dangerous than over-estimating (leading to under-selling) — it is better to sell little profitable business than much loss-making business.

key_terms.definition: Standard premium rates applied to all policyholders without reference to their individual past claims experience. Contrasts with experience rating, typically used for group business.
The degree to which a company's product is differentiated from competitors. A high USP allows greater pricing margins.

subsections.title: Reserving Basis Principles
bullets: The starting point is again best estimate assumptions; margins are then added depending on the purpose of the valuation.
Published accounts: must comply with local legislation and accounting principles; consider going-concern vs break-up basis, true-and-fair view, and required level of prudence.

Solvency accounts: may have specific regulatory constraints on assumptions (eg minimum valuation interest rate = pricing interest rate) or more general constraints (eg result must not be lower than a prescribed basis).
Some regimes (including EU/Solvency II) use best estimate base reserves with a separate risk margin — not embedding margins in assumptions.
Two approaches to statutory reserves: (1) prudent assumptions embedded throughout; (2) best estimate assumptions with a separate risk margin and minimum solvency capital requirement.
Best estimate reserves are used for: internal management accounts; sale of business; assessment of reinsurance; investment strategy. For these, there is no elimination of negative reserves.

Statutory reserving vs pricing: where pricing uses realistic assumptions (risk allowed for via risk discount rate), the same basis cannot be used for prudent statutory reserves — a separate, more cautious reserving basis is needed.

key_terms.definition: An assumption that the insurance company will continue to operate, servicing existing and new business.
An assumption that the insurer continues to service in-force business but writes no new business.
An assumption that the insurer is wound up; existing policies with unexpired cover are cancelled (possibly with return of part of the premium).

subsections.title: Embedded Value and Appraisal Value
bullets: Embedded value (EV) = shareholders' share of net assets + present value of future shareholder profits. It requires two separate bases: a reserving basis and a projection basis.
The risk discount rate for EV calculations is typically lower than for pricing, because new business volume/mix uncertainty does not apply to existing business.
EV for internal management accounts and published accounts: likely to use a realistic/best estimate basis.
EV for sale (appraisal value): seller uses realistic assumptions; purchaser uses cautious assumptions — the difference reflects bargaining power.

An allowance for risk must be included in EV — either through a higher risk discount rate (to reflect risky future profits) or through a market-consistent approach (risk-free discount rate with a separate cost-of-capital risk margin).

A market-consistent EV uses the risk-free rate as the discount rate, assumes investments earn the risk-free rate, and deducts a risk margin for non-investment risks using the cost of capital approach.

key_terms.definition: The value of the future profit stream from existing business plus the net assets attributable to shareholders. Considered the best measure of an insurer's real worth.
Embedded value plus the value of expected future new business (goodwill). Used when valuing an insurer for acquisition.

subsections.title: Data, Grouping, Calculation and Adjustment
bullets: The process: (1) collect appropriate data for a suitable period (adequate volume without excessive heterogeneity); (2) group into relevant homogeneous risk cells; (3) calculate rates — often as an adjustment to a standard table; (4) adjust for differences between the data and the expected future experience.
Morbidity investigations are typically based on 3–4 years of data — balancing the desire for large data volumes against the introduction of excessive heterogeneity from different cohorts of lives.

Rates are commonly derived as an adjustment to a standard table: ix = a·ix(S) + b, where ix(S) is the standard table rate at age x, and a and b are chosen constants. This is simpler, more reliable, and enables ongoing comparison with industry experience.

The key risk factors (subdivisions) for long-term health and care products: age; gender; smoker status; occupation; size of benefit; class of product; individual vs group; distribution channel; region; duration since policy entry; underwriting status.
For PMI and some other short-term products: gender and benefit size are less commonly used as rating factors; age (sometimes in bands) is the primary rating factor.

key_terms.definition: A collection of risks sharing the same attributes across all factors that influence morbidity (eg male, aged 45, smoker, professional occupation).
The aggregate time during which policyholders were exposed to the risk of an event (eg claim inception). Used as the denominator when estimating transition rates.

subsections.title: Incidence Rates by Product
bullets: IP: single rates of transition from healthy to claiming, projected allowing for trends.

CI: separate rates derived for each main illness type (cancer, heart attack, stroke, CABG, TPD, etc), then combined. Individual rates must be projected separately before combining — as trends can differ by illness.
LTCI: rates of transition from able to claiming, and from lower to higher benefit levels. No return transitions (recovery is generally ignored).
PMI: incidence rates and average claim costs assessed separately for each benefit/treatment category.

Social and economic influences on incidence: State welfare generosity (affects PMI and IP demand); economic wellbeing and unemployment (worsens IP claim inception rates and recovery rates); inflation (increases PMI claim costs, may reduce renewal rates).

Lapse and re-entry risk: when premiums fall (eg due to competition), healthy lives lapse and re-enter at cheaper rates, leaving only unhealthy lives at the original price — lowers overall portfolio profitability.

subsections.title: Recovery Rate Factors
bullets: Recovery rates are crucial for IP insurance (and relevant to LTCI, though recovery is rarely assumed there).

Recovery rates typically decrease with increasing duration since sickness onset — the majority of claims are short (<6 months); those still claiming after 6 months often remain claiming for long periods.
Recovery rates also decrease with increasing duration since start of claim payments, for similar reasons.
Longer deferred periods are associated with lower recovery rates after claim inception — only the more seriously ill survive to claim.
Claims for large benefit amounts tend to continue longer than small claims.
Medical advances can aid speedier recovery or prolong life in disability — overall effect on claim costs is ambiguous.

Key factors for recovery: motivation and financial incentive to return to work (replacement ratio); availability of suitable work (economic conditions); claims management and rehabilitation by the insurer.

key_terms.definition: The ratio of post-disability net income (benefits + any other income) to pre-disability net income. High replacement ratios reduce the incentive to return to work, worsening recovery rates.

subsections.title: Significance of Mortality
bullets: Separate mortality rates are needed for: healthy (non-claiming) lives; lives in claim under IP and LTCI; and within the CI survival period.

For non-claiming lives: mortality is typically based on a standard table with adjustments for future improvements. The assumption is relatively unimportant unless there is a significant death benefit.

For IP and LTCI claims in payment: the assumption is more important — sick lives have much higher mortality than healthy lives, and the duration of claim payment is directly affected. More research effort is warranted.
For CI survival period: the effect of mortality post-diagnosis is typically allowed for by an adjustment to the CI claim incidence rate, specific to each illness type.

For policies without a death benefit: it is important not to over-estimate mortality, as death reduces the probability of future health claims — over-estimating mortality would understate expected claim costs.
Medical advances generally reduce mortality — this can increase LTCI and IP claim costs (claims last longer) while having a smaller impact on CI claim incidence.

key_terms.definition: A policy condition requiring a CI claimant to survive for a specified period (eg 28 days) following diagnosis before a claim is payable. Reduces the insurer's exposure to near-immediately terminal illnesses.

subsections.title: Lapse Rate Assumptions
bullets: Lapse rates are based primarily on the insurer's own recent experience, as market data is much less useful — lapse rates are more unpredictable (influenced by human choice), more heterogeneous between insurers, and more sensitive to economic/commercial factors.
No population data exists for lapses — there is no population equivalent of stopping premium payments.

Lapse rates matter most when: they occur early in the policy term (financial loss when asset share is negative); a surrender value is payable; lapses are selective (healthier lives lapse, leaving a worse risk pool); or there is a lapse-and-re-entry risk.
Selective lapsing: for PMI, the lowest claiming members have the highest lapse rates — higher claiming members value the cover more and can less easily obtain it elsewhere.
Factors influencing lapses: economic (affordability), political (State benefit alternatives), commercial (competitor products), awareness (perceived need).
For policies without surrender values and with positive asset shares, a lapse benefits the insurer (asset share retained).

Economic deterioration effect: CI — minimal impact (low premiums, no surrender value); pre-funded LTCI — some increase in withdrawals (higher premiums, possible paid-up options); PMI — significant increase in non-renewals (high premiums, annual decision to renew).

key_terms.definition: Where policyholders lapse existing policies and take out new ones at current lower premium rates. Only healthy lives can benefit, leaving the insurer with deteriorating risk in the original portfolio.
The tendency for lower-risk (healthier) lives to lapse at a higher rate than higher-risk lives, leaving a deteriorating risk pool among those remaining.

subsections.title: Benefit Amount
bullets: Long-term contracts (IP, CI, LTCI): benefit level is fixed in policy terms; premiums are expressed as a rate per unit of sum insured. Only for very large policies would the insurer vary assumptions (due to stricter underwriting and better socio-economic profile of claimants).

PMI: the actuary must forecast both the number and amount of each claim. Average claim amounts are based on own experience or a similar insurer's data, adjusted for: differences in policy conditions; differences in negotiated hospital contracts; and future trends in provider capacity and charges.
Alternatively, private hospital treatment price lists can be used as a starting point for PMI claim amounts.

subsections.title: Benefit Inflation
bullets: Long-term contracts: benefits are often index-linked (eg consumer price index, earnings index, fixed percentage). Even with fixed-rate increases, a model must project the inflated benefit amounts throughout the term.

Best index for IP: national earnings index (replaces lost employment income). For CI: various options (care costs, debt repayment, earnings — in practice often fixed). For LTCI: nursing and residential care cost index.
PMI benefit inflation: depends on medical inflation, changes in treatment protocols, hospital charging structures, age profile of portfolio, and provider capacity.
Many insurers cap benefit inflation (especially LTCI) to contain costs within affordable premiums.

subsections.title: Expense Assumptions
bullets: Expense types: initial acquisition (commission, marketing); initial medical underwriting; initial administration; renewal administration; renewal commission; investment; withdrawal; claim administration; termination.

PMI has the highest renewal administration costs (full premium review each year with potential change in conditions). Investment and withdrawal expenses are unimportant for PMI (short-term, minimal reserves).

Health and care insurance generally has higher expenses than life insurance due to: lower non-medical limits (more applications requiring full underwriting); product complexity (more selling effort); higher claims management costs.

Per-policy expenses do not vary with benefit size. Since premiums are expressed per unit of benefit, smaller policies cross-subsidise larger ones. Solutions: individual premium calculations; policy fee addition; sum insured differential (different rates for different benefit size bands).
The most common approach is a policy fee addition, sometimes combined with a sum insured differential.
Expense assumptions based on own expense analysis; if insufficient, use industry or reinsurers' data.

key_terms.definition: The maximum benefit level for which an application can be accepted without automatically requiring a medical examination or private medical attendant's report.
A flat addition to the premium (or deduction from benefits) used to cover per-policy expenses that do not vary with the sum insured.

subsections.title: Expense Inflation
bullets: Expense inflation must be allowed for, especially for long-term products — expenses are typically a mix of price inflation and salary (earnings) inflation.

Relevant considerations: current price and earnings inflation rates; expected future rates; differential between nominal and index-linked government bond yields; recent actual company/industry experience.
Two aspects of expense inflation when pricing: inflation of expenses during the policy term itself; and inflation of all expenses between now and the dates when future policies will be issued.
An inflation assumption must be made even for reviewable contracts, as the insurer commits to cover being provided and expenses will increase over time.

subsections.title: Commission and Clawback Assumptions
bullets: Commission rates reflect current market levels and any legislative requirements. Commission is generally higher for health and care than for life insurance, due to greater product complexity and underwriting effort.
Commission can be expressed as: a percentage of premium; a percentage of sum insured; or a fixed amount.

Commission clawback: where initial commission is higher than renewal commission and the policy lapses early, the insurer can clawback unearned commission. Clawback assumptions must allow for the credit risk of the intermediary (possibility of default).
The actuary must estimate early lapse rates and the proportion of unearned commission that will actually be reclaimed when setting the pricing basis.
If multiple distributors receive different commission rates for the same product, a weighted average based on expected volumes by channel is required.

key_terms.definition: The recovery by the insurer of part of the initial commission paid to a salesperson when a policy lapses before the commission is deemed 'earned'. The insurer bears a credit risk that the intermediary cannot repay.

subsections.title: Investment Return Assumption
bullets: The investment return assumption must be consistent with inflation assumptions — the implied real return should be realistic.
The significance of the investment return assumption depends on: the size of reserves relative to cashflows; the duration of the policy; and the extent of any investment guarantees.
For regular premium CI insurance: not very important (small reserves, profits insensitive to investment return).
For pre-funded LTCI: very important — especially single premium, where reserves are very large for a long period.
For PMI: of almost no importance (one-year policies, minimal reserves).

The intended investment mix matters: for fixed benefits, fixed-interest bonds are most appropriate; for inflation-linked benefits, index-linked bonds; for equity-like benefit growth (LTCI care costs), some equity investment is appropriate.

Reinvestment risk: the uncertainty of the return from future investment. High for regular premium LTCI (cannot match all liabilities until late in term); low for single premium LTCI (better matching is possible); negligible for PMI.
Under a market-consistent approach, the risk-free rate is used as the discount rate regardless of actual assets held (risk-neutral calibration).

key_terms.definition: The uncertainty of the return that can be obtained from investing future positive cashflows. More important when the policy term is long and liabilities are difficult to match with existing assets.

subsections.title: Tax Assumptions
bullets: Tax rates assumed based on current rates plus known future changes.

Three main types: (1) taxes on profits — the insurer's profit is taxed; premiums must be set to achieve the required post-tax return; (2) taxes on investment income less expenses (I–E basis) — the insurer includes investment income and expenses net of tax; (3) taxes on premiums — the insurer collects tax from policyholders on the State's behalf; indirect impact on lapse/non-renewal assumptions.

key_terms.definition: A tax method where the insurer is taxed on the excess of investment income over allowable expenses. Each item of investment income and expenses is included net of the relevant tax rate.

exam_qa.q: Discuss the importance of the investment return, expense and withdrawal rate assumptions when pricing: (i) without-profits CI insurance; (ii) without-profits immediate needs LTC annuity.
exam_qa.a: (i) CI insurance: Investment return — not very important; reserves are small. Expenses — important; initial underwriting and claim administration costs are relatively high for CI; per-policy expenses must be allocated carefully. Withdrawals — important at early durations (when asset share is negative), a financial loss arises. Lapse and re-entry risk is also relevant if the market becomes more competitive. (ii) Immediate needs LTC annuity: Investment return — very important; these are single-premium policies with large, long-duration reserves; matching assets closely to liabilities is critical; reinvestment risk is relatively low since it is a single premium with immediate claim payments. Expenses — important, especially claim administration for LTCI with multiple benefit levels; but lower than for pre-funded LTCI as no initial underwriting. Withdrawals — not applicable; surrender is typically not permitted for immediate needs LTCI annuities.

subsections.title: New Business Volume
bullets: New business volume assumptions are needed primarily for pricing (to allocate per-policy contributions to fixed expenses) and to assess the capital required to support new sales (new business strain).
For reserving and profitability assessment, actual volumes and mix are known — new business volume assumptions are not required.

subsections.title: New Business Mix
bullets: Mix assumptions are needed when the insurer charges the same premium to heterogeneous groups — if the actual mix differs from assumed, cross-subsidies become losses or gains.
Anti-selection risk: competitors with more detailed risk subdivisions will attract the lighter risks, leaving the insurer with a higher-risk portfolio than assumed.
Mix assumptions are needed by: average policy size; product type; distribution channel; territory; gender (where unisex pricing is required); occupation.

Average policy size affects: the allocation of per-policy expenses; and expected claims experience (larger policies tend to have better claim experience due to stricter underwriting and higher socio-economic profile).
Mix by distribution channel is crucial where different commission rates apply but the same tariff is charged across channels.
Mix by territory: important where health and care risk varies materially by region (eg heavy industrial urban vs agricultural rural for IP).

key_terms.definition: The distribution of new policyholders across different risk categories (age, gender, occupation, distribution channel etc). Errors in assumed mix are a key source of pricing risk.

subsections.title: Components of the Risk Discount Rate
bullets: The risk discount rate reflects: (1) the return required by shareholders (risk-free rate + risk premium for investing in the insurer); and (2) the level of statistical risk attaching to the cashflows under the specific product.

Shareholders' required return is often assessed using the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM): E(i) = r + β·(E(m) − r), where r is the risk-free rate, E(m) is the expected market return, and β (beta) measures the riskiness of the company's shares relative to the market.
Beta > 1 implies the share moves more than the market (higher risk, higher required return). Beta is a measure of systematic (non-diversifiable) risk only.
The overall required return Ei from CAPM is not used directly — products that are more risky than average require a higher discount rate than Ei; less risky products require a lower one.
Product-specific (statistical) risk is assessed via: analytical methods (if variance of parameters can be derived); sensitivity analysis; stochastic modelling; or comparison with market data.
Key features that increase product risk (raising the discount rate): lack of historical data; high guarantees; policyholder options; high overhead costs; complexity of design; untested market.
Key features that decrease product risk: abundant reliable data; reviewable premiums; no policyholder options; low overheads; simple benefits; well-established market.

Important principles: the risk discount rate must exceed the risk-free rate; the margin over the risk-free rate must reflect all sources of risk; overall return on company capital must meet the required return (cost of capital).

key_terms.definition: E(i) = r + β·(E(m) − r). Used to estimate the expected return investors require for a risky asset relative to the risk-free rate. Beta measures systematic risk.
The covariance of a share's return with the market return, divided by the variance of the market return. A measure of an asset's systematic risk relative to the market.
The risk arising from the variability of cashflow components in a pricing model. Assessed through sensitivity analysis, stochastic models, or analytical methods.

subsections.title: Market-Consistent Approach
bullets: An alternative to the CAPM/statistical approach: use risk-free interest rates as the discount rate (risk-neutral calibration), with margins included in each parameter assumption to reflect the inherent risks in their estimation.
The risk margin under market-consistent approaches reflects the compensation required by the 'market' for taking on uncertain aspects of the liability cashflows.
Market-consistent valuations are also useful as a reasonableness check on traditional approaches and for verifying that risk margins are appropriate.

key_terms.definition: A valuation that uses risk-free discount rates (reflecting the risk-neutral measure) and includes explicit risk margins in parameter assumptions. Useful for products with options and guarantees.

exam_qa.q: What is the risk discount rate, and what two main factors should it reflect?
exam_qa.a: The risk discount rate is the rate used to discount net projected cashflows in a profit test, ensuring the expected return meets shareholders' requirements. It should reflect: (1) the return required by shareholders — the risk-free rate plus a risk premium for investing in the insurance company (often quantified via CAPM); and (2) the statistical risk attaching to the cashflows of the specific product — products that are riskier (due to lack of data, high guarantees, complex design, policyholder options, or untested markets) require a higher discount rate.

6.title: Product Design and Stakeholder Interests (1) – Customer Perspective

subsections.title: General Needs and Microinsurance
bullets: From a customer viewpoint, a good product should: meet needs; be clear in purpose (risks covered and benefits payable); have an appropriate charging structure; and above all have an affordable premium that offers value for money.
Health and care insurance products meet three specific needs: providing a lump sum to repay loans; replacing income; and financing medical treatment bills.

IP insurance: meets the need for replacement income when policyholders are unable to work. Benefits are typically limited to 60–75% of gross salary to maintain an incentive to return to work. Main buyers: self-employed individuals, employers (group IP), and small professional practices (eg Locum Protection insurance).

CI insurance: meets a variety of needs — repaying a mortgage or loan; funding medical costs; providing income via annuity; funding business partner buyout (Keyperson cover); lifestyle change after illness. Tiered benefit CI is a closer fit to medical distress and financial need, and is deemed fairer, but is more complex.

LTCI: meets the need for financial protection against costs of care when unable to look after oneself. Care needs progress from domestic support → live-in care → residential care → medical care. Demand depends on adequacy of State and informal (family/friends) provision.

PMI: assists the policyholder in covering the cost of medical care — complementing or replacing State provision. In markets with State provision, PMI is bought for shorter waiting times, better accommodation, choice of doctor, and local hospital access.

Microinsurance: targeted at those with low incomes (especially in developing world). Characterised by limited benefits and very low premiums. Main products: versions of PMI (hospitalisation, primary care) and IP. Often sold alongside microfinance.

key_terms.definition: A CI or life insurance policy taken out by business partners on each other's lives, where the benefit funds the buyout of the sick partner's stake in the business.
Insurance targeted at those with low incomes, characterised by very low premiums and limited benefits. Common in developing markets such as India and parts of Africa.
A CI product design where the sum insured varies according to the severity of the diagnosed illness, providing a closer match to the financial need and allowing cover for less severe conditions.

exam_qa.q: In what sense could you argue that PMI is more focused, and IP more comprehensive, than CI?
exam_qa.a: PMI is more focused: it is an indemnity product that pays for actual medical treatment expenses incurred — the payment is directly related to the actual loss. CI is less focused as it pays a fixed lump sum regardless of the actual loss suffered. IP is more comprehensive: it pays whenever the policyholder cannot work in their own occupation due to any illness or injury, whereas CI only covers specific pre-defined critical illnesses. For example, a broken arm preventing a carpenter from working would trigger an IP benefit but not a CI or PMI payment.

subsections.title: Cash vs Indemnity by Product
bullets: Cash benefits give the policyholder choice — they can be applied to any personal need, converted to income or used for any purpose. However, the customer must understand the likely costs to set an appropriate benefit level.

Indemnity benefits are directly related to the actual loss. An advantage is that the insurer may negotiate cheaper prices from healthcare providers due to volume, making treatment cheaper than if funded from a CI cash benefit.

IP: cash benefit, paid as income stream. May include a rehabilitation or proportional benefit to encourage return to work — benefiting both insurer (lower claim costs) and policyholder (faster recovery).

CI: cash benefit, paid as lump sum. The lump sum cannot be subsequently withdrawn by the insurer once paid. Can be more than the immediate cost of care — surplus available for recuperation or lifestyle change. Risk of 'windfall gains' where benefit greatly exceeds loss (eg angioplasty now a minor procedure) — can cause anti-selection and moral hazard.

LTCI: typically cash benefits (income stream). Few insurers offer indemnity benefits due to the difficulty of forecasting future care costs, longevity risk, and benefit inflation uncertainty. Immediate needs plans convert a single premium into a lifetime income at point of need.

PMI: normally an indemnity product — pays actual medical treatment costs, often directly to the provider. Exceptions include: health cash plans (partial cash contribution); daily benefit for use of State hospital; major medical expense plans (lump sum for surgery); personal accident plans.

key_terms.definition: A benefit that reimburses the actual costs incurred (eg treatment expenses), returning the policyholder to the financial position they were in before the insured event. Contrasts with a cash benefit of fixed amount.
A type of PMI variant that provides a partial cash contribution when certain medical events occur (eg physiotherapy, new spectacles), rather than full indemnification. Reduces anti-selection risk.

subsections.title: Peace of Mind by Product
bullets: Customers are only concerned about risks they recognise — many are unaware of the true likelihood of long-term sickness vs critical illness, leading CI to outsell IP in some markets.
IP: concern about being unable to provide for dependants or repay loans. State sickness benefits are often inadequate and employer benefits are usually short-term.
CI: provides financial peace of mind, but is not comprehensive — it only covers specific headline illnesses. A combination of IP and PMI may be better targeted and more comprehensive.

LTCI: concerns about insufficient funds for care in old age and inadequacy of State/informal provision. Key issue — policyholders may believe care is being bought, when only cash is provided. Some insurers address this with a care management advisory service.
PMI: concern about unaffordable treatment costs, waiting times, or quality of State provision. Employer may also share the concern about employees being unable to work.

subsections.title: Simplicity and Clarity
bullets: IP: simple concept but complications arise from: benefit limits (replacement ratio cap); payouts not always linked to current salary; complex underwriting (occupation, medical history); exclusions (alcohol, drug abuse, self-inflicted injury, war); difficulty defining incapacity objectively. ADLs, ADWs, FATs and PCAs are objective tests used.

CI: theoretically simple (fixed lump sum on diagnosis). Attractions: lump sum cannot be withdrawn; claims trigger (diagnosis or procedure) is easily explained. But: exclusions and point-of-claim underwriting complicate matters; claim definitions may differ between insurers or require evidence of severity beyond the layperson's understanding. Tiered CI is more complex — greater potential for claims dispute.

LTCI: concept is straightforward but confusion arises over: whether cash benefits will meet actual care costs; benefit caps or maximum payment periods; trigger points (ADLs, cognitive impairment) need explanation.

PMI: indemnification is clear in concept but complications arise from: underwriting/acceptance process; exclusions (chronic and pre-existing conditions, cosmetic surgery); cost-sharing (coinsurance, excesses/deductibles); pre-authorisation requirements; restrictions to hospital bands or networks.

key_terms.definition: A set of defined personal care activities (eg bathing, dressing, eating, mobility) used as objective trigger criteria for LTCI and some IP claims. Inability to perform a specified number of ADLs without assistance triggers the claim.
A PMI requirement for the insurer to approve a planned procedure before treatment begins, ensuring it is covered by the policy and performed in an appropriate facility. Reduces disputed claims.
A cost-sharing arrangement under which the policyholder pays a fixed percentage of every claim cost, with the insurer covering the remainder.

sections.title: Guaranteed vs Reviewable Premiums and Benefits

subsections.title: Guarantees and Reviewability by Product
bullets: Policyholders prefer guaranteed premiums (certainty of future outgo) but guarantees are expensive to price and reserve for, especially in health and care insurance where there are many factors outside the insurer's control.

IP: premiums may be guaranteed or periodically reviewable. Claims experience is affected by economic and social influences (eg unemployment trends). Selective lapsing limits the size of any premium increase under reviewable policies — capping increases at new business rates helps retention.

CI: originally premiums were reviewable due to unknown future risks. Competition pushed some insurers to offer guaranteed premiums at little extra cost, but the true reserving cost is now better understood. With many illness trends moving in different directions, future premium increases under reviews are difficult to predict.

LTCI — immediate needs: no premium guarantee issues — single premium buys a lifetime income stream. LTCI — pre-funded: reviewable premiums predominate. Degree of variation depends on claims experience and trend in care cost index.

PMI: almost invariably a one-year product, annually renewable. Premiums (and sometimes policy terms) can change at each renewal. Guaranteed premium products for limited periods (3–5 years) are rare and only viable when medical inflation is stable.

key_terms.definition: Premiums that cannot be changed by the insurer throughout the policy term. Provide certainty to the policyholder but are expensive to price, as the insurer must hold sufficient reserves to cover all future adverse scenarios.
Premiums that the insurer may change at defined review dates, based on the emerging experience of the whole portfolio. The policyholder retains the option to accept or decline the new terms.

exam_qa.q: Outline the factors that influence the suitability of offering guaranteed vs reviewable premiums on long-term health and care insurance business.
exam_qa.a: Factors favouring guaranteed premiums: customer preference for certainty; competitive pressure (if competitors offer guarantees); simpler product (easier to sell); less risk of selective lapsing on review. Factors favouring reviewable premiums: less need for prudent reserves upfront (cheaper premium); greater ability to respond to adverse experience (eg new diseases, medical advances for CI); economic and social influences on IP claims are hard to predict long-term; for PMI, claims inflation is inherently unpredictable over multiple years; if the insurer has limited capital, smaller reserves required for reviewable products; where data are scarce, pricing is uncertain and review allows correction. In practice, the insurer must balance cost competitiveness, policyholder expectations, regulatory requirements, and its own risk appetite.

7.title: Product Design and Stakeholder Interests (2) – Insurer, Regulator and Other Stakeholders

subsections.title: Insurer Product Design Considerations
bullets: Product design and pricing is an iterative procedure balancing: customer acceptability; regulator requirements; needs of distributors; price competitiveness; adequate profitability/return on capital; company culture in product style and price; systems and other internal constraints; and underwriting methodology.

Customer acceptability: the product must be clear about both the benefits provided (claims triggers, cash values) and the amounts and variability of premiums. Innovative features and options increase marketability but must be understood by the target customer base — simplicity matters for the chosen distribution channel.
Adequate profitability: premiums must cover benefits, expenses, and leave a surplus to reward shareholders. The balance between competitiveness and profitability is a fundamental management task.

Company culture: new product structures should be similar to existing products to avoid systems development costs, staff retraining, and the risk of dissatisfying existing policyholders who may feel unfairly treated.

Systems constraints: IT must record all processes, provide profitability information, and be capable of handling the product design. Systems limitations may restrict product features. Data capture is critical for pricing, monitoring, and repricing.

Underwriting methodology: medical underwriting is a fundamental element of product design. For long-term guaranteed premium policies, stringent underwriting is most important as there is no opportunity to revise terms. For one-year renewable policies (PMI), moratorium underwriting (collecting evidence only at claim) reduces expense but increases anti-selection risk.
Claims procedures must be consistent with underwriting criteria and pricing data. They should match policyholder expectations and broadly match competitor practices to avoid adverse anti-selection.

Risk pricing: a clear set of conditions and policy rules enables statistical costing and controls anti-selection and moral hazard. Reinsurer assistance is often vital for data, contract design, and pricing, especially for new products.

Financing requirement: the insurer will want to minimise its capital strain on new business. Guarantees have a big influence on up-front reserve requirements. This is particularly important for relatively new health and care insurers with limited free assets.

Cross-subsidies: where premium averaging is needed, the actuary must monitor the mix of business to ensure cross-subsidies do not create systematic losses. The rights of different policyholder classes must not be diminished.

key_terms.definition: The tendency for those with a higher-than-average risk to seek insurance cover, while lower-risk individuals decline. Arises when the insurer does not capture all relevant risk information. Also known as adverse selection.
The risk that a policyholder's behaviour changes adversely as a result of having insurance — eg taking greater risks or using more healthcare services than they otherwise would.

A PMI underwriting approach where medical evidence of existing conditions is only collected at the point of claim, rather than at inception. Reduces initial expense but increases the risk of accepting sub-standard risks.

The initial negative cashflow arising when a new policy is written, equal to the initial supervisory reserve plus solvency capital plus initial expenses minus the first premium. Must be financed from free assets.

exam_qa.q: Why is reinsurer expertise more important for health and care insurance products than for life insurance protection products?
exam_qa.a: Health and care insurance involves greater complexity — incidence rates are harder to predict, claim definitions are more subjective, and experience data are scarcer. When launching a new health and care product (eg CI in a new market), a reinsurer can provide: statistics from other markets; relevant population data; actuarial and pricing expertise; assistance with contract wording; regulatory knowledge; and training for internal staff. Reinsurers accumulate experience across many insurers and territories, making their data particularly valuable in markets with limited historical data. For standard life insurance protection (term assurance), mortality data are well established and standard tables are widely available, making external reinsurer expertise less critical.

sections.title: Product-Specific Control Procedures for the Insurer

subsections.title: Control by Product
bullets: IP: initial underwriting/acceptance with premiums reflecting risk factors (occupation, age, gender where permitted); claims underwriting (checking benefit against current salary); use of claims counsellors; monitoring by specialist nurses throughout claims.

CI: effective underwriting and claims control to guard against anti-selection and non-disclosure (especially important for tiered benefit CI); survival period on stand-alone policies (avoids 'death' claims); adequate IBNR reserves for late-notified claims; managing policyholder expectations as new diseases emerge; careful policy wording (especially for tiered benefits); large margins and/or reinsurer co-operation where data are scarce.
LTCI: policy designs must be robust to changes in State provision of care and regulatory interpretation of benefits promised.
PMI: control of claim costs by agreeing and monitoring provider prices; pre-authorisation of benefits; ensuring premium increases can be justified to policyholders at renewal.

key_terms.definition: Claims that have been incurred (the insured event has occurred) but have not yet been formally notified to the insurer. Reserves must be held to cover expected IBNR claims.

subsections.title: Regulatory and Other Stakeholder Interests
bullets: Regulators' priorities: (1) the insurer remains solvent; (2) consumers are considered in all decisions and disadvantages to consumers in sales, administration, and claims are minimised.

Regulatory requirements may include: adequate and accurate reporting to the regulator; approval of premium increases; clear and unambiguous policy wording; a range of suitable benefits representing reasonable value for money; monitoring of sales processes and target market appropriateness.

Distributors (intermediaries, tied agents, own salesforce): interested in having products that are easy to explain, competitively priced, and carry sufficient commission. The sales incentive must be sufficient to encourage distribution but appropriate from a regulatory perspective.

Actuarial profession: professional guidance gives actuaries a framework for maintaining professional standards and may provide protection against pressure from management. Guidance typically covers: policy conditions; adequacy of premium rates; value of the liabilities. Professional guidance must not unduly restrict actuaries' judgement.

Good product design rule of thumb: ensure there is a customer need; price accurately, projecting a range of outcomes; charge the cost of capital to the product; obtain sound reinsurance; ensure policy literature clearly describes guarantees; ensure adequate reserves are in place.

subsections.title: Four Channels and Worksite Marketing
bullets: Insurance intermediaries (brokers): act independently of any particular insurer; find the contract that best meets the client's needs in terms of benefits and premiums; remunerated by commission from insurers or fees from clients. Clients usually initiate the sale. Risk of mis-selling if intermediaries are motivated by commission levels rather than client suitability.

Tied agents: tied to one (or sometimes a few) insurer(s); offer only those insurers' products (ranges are usually mutually exclusive). Typically employees of a bank or other financial institution — bancassurance. Remunerated by commission from the insurer. Active selling is common. Product ranges of multiple companies are usually mutually exclusive to avoid conflicts.

Own salesforce: employees of the insurer, selling only that insurer's products. Remunerated by salary, commission, or a mixture. Sales are usually initiated by the salesperson. Benefits from having a 'warm' client base once established.

Direct marketing: four forms — mailshots (insurer initiates); telephone selling (either party initiates); press advertising (debatable); internet/comparison websites (usually client initiates). Internet enables basic underwriting at quotation stage. Simple products (cash plans, basic PMI) are most suitable for internet distribution.

Worksite marketing: a broker or insurer representative obtains permission from an employer to address the entire workforce. Intended to sell simple products to individuals who lack cover; these are individual (not group) policies and continue independently of employment. LTCI is unsuitable for worksite marketing (too complex, post-employment need, expensive). CI, IP, and health cash plans are suitable.

key_terms.definition: The distribution of insurance products through a bank's distribution network. The bank acts as a tied agent for its associated insurance company, marketing products to its existing customer base.

A distribution approach where an insurer or broker obtains permission from an employer to address the workforce and sell individual health and care insurance products, using the workplace as a convenient point of access.

subsections.title: Types of Commission
bullets: Initial and renewal commission: high initial commission for a defined initial period (eg 24 months), then a lower renewal commission thereafter — both expressed as percentages of premiums in the same period. PMI equivalents: higher commission for new policies vs renewals.

Indemnity commission: a form of initial commission — a lump sum paid immediately when the policy is sold (typically as a percentage of the first premium). Involves new business strain as commission is paid before premiums are fully received. A credit check on the distributor is required.

Clawback: the repayment of commission by the intermediary if a policy lapses before the commission is fully earned. The insurer bears a credit risk that the intermediary may be unable to repay (eg through bankruptcy). The proportion clawed back typically varies with the premiums actually paid relative to those expected.

Renewal commission: a lesser ongoing amount paid after the initial period to encourage persistency; paid annually on long-term policies after the earnings period, or annually on renewal for short-term policies.

Level commission: a fixed proportion of every premium paid, throughout the policy term. Does not involve new business strain; matches commission outgo to premium contributions and profitability. Less popular with intermediaries (lower initial payment) but more suitable for the insurer's cashflow.
Commission may also be expressed as a proportion of the sum insured (rather than the premium), which better reflects the insurer's preference for higher sums at risk.

key_terms.definition: A lump sum commission paid to the distributor immediately on new business being written, representing an advance against future premiums. Involves new business strain and a credit risk on the distributor.
The recovery by the insurer of part of the initial commission paid to a salesperson when a policy lapses before the commission is fully earned. Subject to credit risk if the intermediary defaults.
A commission structure where a fixed percentage of every premium is paid to the distributor throughout the policy term. No new business strain; better aligns commission outgo with profitability.

subsections.title: Impact on Experience and Risk
bullets: Different channels appeal to different customers based on financial sophistication and income. The sophistication of the client base influences the complexity of products that can be sold.

Claims and persistency experience will vary by channel, depending on: financial sophistication; income levels; level of underwriting applied; and who initiated the sale (customer-initiated sales tend to have better experience as the policyholder has a genuine perceived need).
Greatest competition — and hence pressure for low premiums — exists in business sold through insurance brokers and internet comparison websites.
Worksite marketing: benefits from the prospect of engaging a large number of similar risks (keeping risk premiums low) and possible expense savings; limited by small average policy sizes.

Group risks via brokers: the broker handles communication, data gathering, and transfers. The insurer benefits from assured quality of selection and minimal additional administrative expense, but has limited ability to: build a relationship with the employer; influence retention of the business; or influence the employer's risk management.

sections.title: Propensity to Buy vs Need to Sell

subsections.title: Bought vs Sold Products
bullets: Insurance that is legally compulsory is 'bought'. Insurance that is neither legally required nor obviously needed must be 'sold' — the intermediary must first make the customer aware of the need.

IP: should be a need for most, but is often not perceived as such. Many people are unaware of the inadequacy of State sickness benefits. Primarily a 'sold' product. Short-term IP may be legally binding on employers.

CI: not directly matched to a specific financial need; provides a lump sum at time of crisis. The 'lottery syndrome' (attraction of a large payout) means it is sometimes 'bought'. Provides peace of mind even if the policyholder can't specify what the money would be used for.

LTCI: need depends on perceived government attitude to care provision and individual priorities. Elements of legal compulsion exist in some countries. The State may incentivise purchase by providing low State provision or means-testing assets before providing care.

PMI: if all medical costs are individual responsibility, PMI is a need. If the State provides free healthcare, PMI must be sold — purchased for shorter waiting times, choice, and better accommodation.

Growing awareness that the State cannot maintain current welfare benefit levels (due to ageing populations, rising medical costs, and fiscal pressures) is increasing the underlying demand for health and care insurance.
Increasing sales regulation and disclosure requirements are reducing insurers' ability to 'create needs' through the sales process — products must genuinely meet customer needs to succeed.

subsections.title: Types of Regulatory Restriction
bullets: The primary aim of regulation is policyholder protection, ensuring public confidence in the insurance industry so that it can continue to attract business.

Regulatory restrictions may include: (1) restrictions on the types of contract an insurer can offer; (2) restrictions on premium rates or charges (eg maximum premium rates); (3) restrictions on rating factors (eg gender, genetic test results); (4) requirements on terms and conditions of contracts (eg claim definitions, maximum frequency and size of premium increases for reviewable products); (5) restrictions on sales channels and procedures (eg cooling-off periods, benefit illustrations, minimum salesperson training requirements); (6) restrictions on underwriting (eg prohibition on use of genetic test results or past claims history); (7) indirect capital constraint via minimum reserving and solvency capital requirements; (8) restrictions on types or amounts of assets used for solvency demonstration.
Regulation may discourage innovation or restrict benefits that could otherwise be provided to policyholders — a trade-off against policyholder protection.

The wider regulatory environment also matters — other institutions (eg hospital networks, care homes) may offer competing products under different regulatory constraints, creating an uneven playing field.

Genetic test results: insurers are generally prohibited from requiring genetic tests (an ethical infringement of personal liberty) or using results in underwriting, though they may continue to collect gender data.

key_terms.definition: A regulatory requirement giving policyholders the right to cancel a new insurance policy within an initial period (eg 30 days) without any financial loss.
The supervisory value of assets minus the supervisory value of liabilities (including SCR). Represents the capital available to write new business. Also called free capital or free reserves.

subsections.title: Taxation Approaches and Contract Design
bullets: Two main approaches to taxing insurance business: (1) tax on annual profits (increase in free assets over the year); (2) tax on investment income/gains less operating expenses (I–E basis).

The I–E basis is less suitable for health and care products than for life insurance, as most health and care products are primarily protection-based with small reserves and little investment income — little or no tax would be payable, which may not be acceptable for proprietary companies.
LTCI is the exception — it has a significant investment component and would generate more tax under an I–E basis.
Premium taxes exist in some territories — the insurer collects these on behalf of the State. Higher premium taxes reduce the attractiveness of insurance, increasing lapse rates.

Different types of business (short-term vs long-term, protection vs savings, conventional vs unit-linked) may be taxed differently within a country. Savings business may be exempt if policyholders receive gross investment returns, with benefits taxed instead.

Tax treatment of premiums (deductibility), investment return, and benefits all affect the overall attractiveness of a product. The State may use tax concessions to incentivise purchase of certain health products (eg tax relief on PMI or LTCI premiums).
Product design aims to make the best use of tax opportunities and must work within any tax-driven constraints (eg minimum insurance cover required to qualify for concessions).

key_terms.definition: A method of taxing insurance companies on investment income and capital gains less operating expenses, rather than on total profits. Common in some long-term insurance markets.

subsections.title: Professional Guidance
bullets: Actuarial associations issue professional guidance to give actuaries a framework for maintaining professional standards in advising health and care insurers.
Guidance typically covers: policy conditions; the adequacy of premium rates for new business; the value of the liabilities.
Guidance may also interpret government regulations where the government does not wish to be overly prescriptive — the profession effectively sets acceptable limits on behaviour.
Professional guidance provides protection to actuaries against pressure from management to agree to courses of action not in the best interests of policyholders.

subsections.title: Economic and Other Influences
bullets: Inflation: benefits must meet both current and future policyholder needs. Fixed level benefits risk becoming inadequate over time. Fixed escalation rates may be insufficient or excessive relative to actual inflation. Index-linked benefits may not match the specific inflation of health/care costs. Indemnity contracts transfer inflation risk to the insurer — especially risky for long-term guaranteed premium indemnity products.
Business cycle: demand for employer-sponsored insurances is higher in business upturns (used as a staff retention tool). In downturns, employers cut benefits or reduce coverage to save costs.

Employment security: high job security increases demand. Low job security increases some claim inceptions — eg employees claim under PMI before cover is lost; employers may encourage IP claims as a way to reduce the salary bill in downturns; work-related stress and depression increase IP claims.

Political stability: some political regimes are more favourable to health and care insurance than others (eg willingness to reduce State provision). Political stability generally indicates: economic wellbeing; stability of regulatory and tax rules; stability of the future economic value of insurance policies.

exam_qa.q: If premium regulation specifies minimum premium rates for CI insurance, what is the regulator's concern, and is this appropriate?
exam_qa.a: The regulator's concern is that excessively low premium rates may threaten the financial security of the insurer and hence the security of its policyholders. Minimum premium rates could prevent insurers from under-pricing and becoming insolvent. However, the regulator might also consider maximum premium rates to prevent overcharging, particularly in markets where competition is limited and consumers may not have access to easily comparable pricing information. Both upper and lower limits are theoretically possible — the appropriate choice depends on market structure and whether effective competition can be relied upon to control excessive pricing.

subsections.title: Four Key Objectives
bullets: Protecting the nation's health: a healthy and productive workforce promotes GDP growth, which funds the healthcare system — a self-reinforcing cycle. The State's role ranges from a market-focused (minimal involvement) to a centrally provident approach (full State responsibility). Key activities include: providing food, water, nutrition and hygiene; basic medical services and education; modern medical facilities; public health education campaigns; funding research and import of health technologies.

Subsidising the poor: even market-focused countries maintain a basic minimum level of provision for the poorest, children, and the aged — as part of the State's attempts to redistribute wealth. Means-tested benefits are paid only to those below a defined income or wealth threshold.

Balancing the budget: healthcare can be funded via specific health charges (eg national insurance) or general taxation or both. Healthcare costs typically rise faster than consumer prices due to: improvements in medical technology; growing public expectations; and mortality improvements leading to more elderly people with greater care needs. This creates ongoing fiscal pressure.

Following social culture and political promises: State healthcare programmes become part of national culture and expectation, making radical changes politically difficult. In the UK, for example, the 'free at point of need' principle of the NHS has persisted across many governments. Role of commercial insurance depends heavily on the nature of State provision.

key_terms.definition: A system where State benefits are only provided to individuals whose income or wealth falls below a defined threshold. Aims to target benefits at those most in need.

sections.title: Models of Co-existence with Private Insurance

subsections.title: Co-existence Models
bullets: Insurance as an optional alternative: State promises comprehensive medical services but insurance provides higher quality, choice of doctor, shorter wait, and better accommodation. Example: UK, where PMI supplements the NHS.
Insurance as an optional complement: State provides limited services; individuals fund the balance (eg USA and Canada).
Insurance as a compulsory alternative: State provides everything up to a salary/wealth threshold; insurance is obligatory for those above it.

Insurance as a compulsory complement: State provides certain procedures free; insurance is mandatory for all other procedures. Example: Germany — most people in gainful employment must be insured under a statutory health scheme, but high earners can opt for private alternatives.
Co-existence applies to direct medical services (covering PMI-type needs) and to income provision when unable to work (covering IP-type needs).

subsections.title: Types of Provision
bullets: Means-tested vs universal: means-tested benefits are paid only in cases of financial hardship. Universal benefits are available to all regardless of wealth.

Direct vs reimbursement provision: the State may operate its own medical establishments (better cost and claim control) or reimburse commercial providers (benefits from expertise, economies of scale, and competitive market pressure).
Lump sum vs regular income: lump sum for capital expenditure needs (eg home adaptation); regular income for ongoing financial support when unable to work.

Flat-rate vs earnings-related: flat-rate benefits are simple to understand and administer, provide an incentive to return to work, and encourage self-provision, but may be unrelated to pre-disability living standards. Earnings-related benefits reward those who have contributed more through taxation and better reflect their financial responsibilities. Many countries use a combination.
Benefits are often index-linked to consumer prices. Benefit levels may differ by severity of disability and by the recipient's circumstances (employed, unemployed, student, pensioner, war veteran).

sections.title: Funding Approaches and Incentives for Self-Provision

subsections.title: PAYG vs Forward Funding
bullets: Pay-As-You-Go (PAYG): the current working population funds the benefits of those currently in need. Process: estimate coming year's outgo and tax revenue; adjust taxation to balance the welfare budget. Risk: as populations age and the ratio of workers to beneficiaries falls, PAYG contributions become increasingly insufficient. Shortfalls must be funded from general tax revenues.

Forward funding: takes a longer-term view — models expected State outgo over a future period (eg 5–20 years); estimates population and workforce trends; forecasts tax revenues; and creates an earmarked healthcare fund to bridge any shortfall. More robust against demographic change but requires long-term political commitment.
In practice, most countries use a combination of PAYG and forward funding, especially as demographic forecasts indicate a declining ratio of workers to beneficiaries.

key_terms.definition: A State healthcare funding method where current workers' contributions fund the benefits of those currently in need, rather than accumulating a fund for future obligations. Vulnerable to ageing population dynamics.
A State healthcare funding approach that models future outgo and builds up an earmarked fund in advance to meet those obligations, rather than relying solely on current contributions.

subsections.title: Incentives for Self-Provision
bullets: The State may encourage self-provision to promote insurance and/or limit State outgo. Methods include: tax relief on healthcare insurance premiums (eg Republic of Ireland for PMI and LTCI); exclusion of some populations from certain State benefits; reduced general taxes where appropriate insurance is in place; direct subsidies to reduce the cost of private healthcare services.

The overall role of insurance in a national healthcare system depends on: historically evolved cultures and expectations; budget constraints; differing perceived priorities for different population segments; and the approach taken to funding and self-provision.

Key insight: the more the State reduces its own provision (or requires means-testing of assets before providing care), the stronger the incentive for individuals to purchase private health and care insurance.

exam_qa.q: Explain how the pay-as-you-go (PAYG) system of funding State healthcare operates and depends on the trust between successive generations.
exam_qa.a: Under PAYG, the current working population pays contributions (via national insurance or taxation) that directly fund the benefits of those currently in need (the sick, elderly, disabled). There is no individual pre-funding — the current generation effectively pays for the previous generation's healthcare costs, in the expectation that a future working generation will do the same for them. This requires inter-generational trust: today's workers must believe that future workers will honour the same obligation to them. If the working population shrinks relative to the number of beneficiaries (as occurs with ageing populations), contributions become insufficient without raising tax rates or reducing benefits — eroding this trust and the sustainability of the system.
